name: FLDS Warren Jeffs
description: Warren Jeffs was the president of the Fundamentalist Church of Jesus Christ of Latter-Day Saints (FLDS Church), a breakaway sect of Mormonism that practices polygamy. The concept surrounding 'FLDS Warren Jeffs' encompasses not just the man himself, but also the theological and societal system he enforced, his legal troubles, and the impact his leadership had on the FLDS community and beyond. Proponents, mainly within the FLDS community during his reign, viewed Jeffs as a prophet, divinely appointed to lead them and interpret God's will. His teachings, often communicated through secretive 'revelations,' emphasized strict obedience to his authority, including arranged marriages and the reallocation of families. Evidence cited by followers included Jeffs's pronouncements and purported miracles or blessings attributed to his guidance. Mainstream scholarly perspectives and legal systems, however, view Jeffs as a religious extremist and criminal. He was convicted of child sexual assault in 2011 for officiating a marriage between a 14-year-old girl and an older man. Critics argue that Jeffs exploited his position of power for personal gain and to control the lives of FLDS members, particularly women and girls. His teachings led to widespread social isolation, economic hardship, and psychological trauma within the community. The legal actions against Jeffs and the FLDS Church highlighted the conflict between religious freedom and the protection of children and vulnerable populations. The story of Warren Jeffs raises complex questions about religious authority, cult dynamics, and the role of the state in regulating religious practices.
subtitle: Polygamy, Power, and the FLDS Church

claims.claim: Warren Jeffs was a divinely appointed prophet.
claims.evidence: Testimonies of FLDS members, purported miracles, and claims of divine revelation.
claims.criticism: Mainstream religious scholars and former FLDS members dismiss these claims, citing Jeffs's criminal actions and manipulative behavior. His pronouncements are viewed as self-serving and not divinely inspired.

claims.claim: Strict obedience to Warren Jeffs was necessary for salvation.
claims.evidence: Jeffs's sermons and teachings emphasizing obedience to his authority as God's chosen leader.
claims.criticism: Critics argue that this teaching created a cult-like environment where members were manipulated and controlled, leading to psychological abuse and social isolation.

claims.claim: Arranged marriages were divinely ordained and necessary for spiritual purity.
claims.evidence: Jeffs's pronouncements and reinterpretation of religious texts to justify arranged marriages, often involving underage girls.
claims.criticism: Legal systems and human rights advocates condemn arranged marriages, especially those involving minors, as violations of human rights and child abuse.

keyFigures.name: Sam Brower
keyFigures.role: Investigative journalist and author of 'Prophet's Prey'

mythologyConnections: Biblical interpretations supporting polygamy (Old Testament figures)
Concept of a divinely appointed leader or prophet

timeline.event: Warren Jeffs becomes president of the FLDS Church after his father's death.
Jeffs is placed on the FBI's 'Ten Most Wanted Fugitives' list.
Jeffs is convicted of child sexual assault and sentenced to life in prison.

sources.author: Sam Brower
sources.work: Prophet's Prey: My Seven-Year Investigation Into Warren Jeffs and the Fundamentalist Church of Latter-Day Saints
sources.title: Sam Brower

sources.author: Jon Krakauer
sources.work: Under the Banner of Heaven: A Story of Violent Faith
sources.title: Jon Krakauer
